coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from Prunus cerasifera fruits for fluorescent ink, Fe3+ ion detection and cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Huipeng
Sun, Cuicui
Xue, Gerilehu
Wu, Guanlin
Zhang, Xiaohan
Han, Xu
Qi, Xiaohui
Lv, Xia
Sun, Huijun
Zhang, Jianbin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from natural products have drawn numerous attentions due to some unique properties. Here, Prunus cerasifera fruits were used as carbon source to synthesize high luminescent CDs by hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs were characterized by TEM, FTIR and XPS methods, founding the CDs were near-spherical and contained abundant nitrogen element. The CDs aqueous solution exhibited bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet illumination, with the maximum emission at 450 nm. They could be potentially used as invisible fluorescent ink by written on the paper and irradiated by UV light, due to their fluorescent properties. Moreover, the CDs were found being selectively quenched by Fe3+ ion. The quench of CDs was linearly related to the concentration of Fe3+ ion in the range of 0–0.5 mM, meaning they could be developed as fluorescent probe of Fe3+ ion. At last, the CDs were used for cell imaging, founding they were low toxicity to HepG2 cells and exhibited blue and green fluorescence under a fluorescence microscope. In summary, the CDs prepared from Prunus cerasifera fruits exhibited excellent fluorescence properties, and could be potentially applied in the field of fluorescent ink, Fe3+ ion detection and cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Prunus cerasifera
Fluorescent ink
Cell imaging
Fe3+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and size distribution of CDs. (B) FTIR spectrum of CDs.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of CDs. The high solution XPS C 1s (B), N 1s (C) and O 1s (D) spectra of CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption (black line), fluorescence excitation (Ex, blue line) and emission (Em, red line) spectra of CDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with different excitation wavelength. (C) Fluorescence intensity of CDs at different pH condition. (D) Photostability of CDs with different storage time.
Digital photographs of the handwritten letters of “Dalian DMU” on the filter paper radiated under daylight and UV light.

(A) Selectivity of CDs on Fe3+ ion over other metal ions, F0 and F present the fluorescence intensity of CDs before and after adding metal ions. (B) The relationship of (F0 − F)/F0 versus the Fe3+ ion concentration. (C) Digital photographs of CDs with or without Fe3+ ion radiated under daylight and UV light.
Cellular viability of HepG2 cells in the present of different concentration of CDs.
Fluorescence images of HepG2 cells incubated with CDs (350 μg/mL) for 12 h, (A) under bright field, (B) under 405 nm excitation, (C) under 488 nm excitation.
Schematic illustration for the preparation of CDs.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from Prunus cerasifera fruits for fluorescent ink, Fe3+ ion detection and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Dalian Medical University, Dalian 116044, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Medical Laboratory, Dalian Medical University, Dalian 116044, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biotechnology and Bioresources Utilization, Ministry of Education, College of Life Science, Dalian Minzu University, Dalian 116600, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biotechnology and Bioresources Utilization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from natural products have drawn numerous attentions due to some unique properties. Here, Prunus cerasifera fruits were used as carbon source to synthesize high luminescent CDs by hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs were characterized by TEM, FTIR and XPS methods, founding the CDs were near-spherical and contained abundant nitrogen element. The CDs aqueous solution exhibited bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet illumination, with the maximum emission at 450 nm. They could be potentially used as invisible fluorescent ink by written on the paper and irradiated by UV light, due to their fluorescent properties. Moreover, the CDs were found being selectively quenched by Fe3+ ion. The quench of CDs was linearly related to the concentration of Fe3+ ion in the range of 0–0.5 mM, meaning they could be developed as fluorescent probe of Fe3+ ion. At last, the CDs were used for cell imaging, founding they were low toxicity to HepG2 cells and exhibited blue and green fluorescence under a fluorescence microscope. In summary, the CDs prepared from Prunus cerasifera fruits exhibited excellent fluorescence properties, and could be potentially applied in the field of fluorescent ink, Fe3+ ion detection and cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial with particle sizes below 10 nm, have drawn numerous attentions of researchers since discovered serendipitously in 2004 [1]. Compared with traditional organic fluorescent dyes and inorganic quantum dots, CDs showed some unique properties, including good biocompatibility, water dispersibility, photostability, environmental friendliness, lower cost, tunable emission, and easy modification [2]. These advantages benefit them to be widely applied in the fields of bioimaging, sensor, drug delivery, fluorescent ink, solar cells, catalysis and so on [3]. Despite the fluorescence-emission mechanism of CDs has not been fully explained, it is generally believed that the fluorescence properties of CDs are related to the surface detects, quantum size effect, surface state effect, edge state and carbon core state effect [4–7]. Moreover, the carbon source, preparation method and conditions are considered as the key factors to affect the CDs properties.

Up to now, CDs are synthesized mainly by “top-down” method and “bottom-up” methods [8]. The “top-down” methods involve the conversion of large particles into smaller nano-size CDs via different methods, including arc-discharge, laser ablation, chemical or electrochemical oxidation methods [9]. These methods always encounter the difficulties of expensive equipment, harsh synthetic conditions, tedious processes as well as high costs for large-scale production. The “bottom-up” methods involve the conversion of smaller particles into CDs of desired size range, such as microwave, hydrothermal and wet chemical methods [10]. Among those methods, hydrothermal method is considered to be a facile, green and efficient approach. The carbon source of hydrothermal method could be man-made materials (e.g. citric acid, ethylenediamine, glucose, graphite and ammonium hydroxide) or natural products (e.g. milk, egg, flowers, fruits, roots and leaves) [5]. Actually, compared with man-made materials, natural products as the carbon source of CDs has the advantages of eco-friendly, abundant source, low cost and rich-in multiple elements. Moreover, some waste biomass can also be converted into valuable CDs. Most importantly, the prepared CDs showed the properties of low toxicity, high photoluminescence and stable, leading them more suitable for biomedical application. Recently, various CDs have been reported using natural products as the carbon sources, such as pear juice [11], tomato juice [12], lemon juice [13,14], quince fruit [15], carrot juice [16], watermelon peel [17], rose flower [18], beet [19], Ocimum sanctum [20], Phyllanthus acidus [21], Curcuma longa [22], Hongcaitai [23], Lycii Fructus [24], and Syringa obtata Lindl [25]. Those CDs could be used for sensors, fluorescent ink, bioimaging and drug delivery. Atchudan et al. reported the highly fluorescent CDs derived from Phyllanthus acidus, which could be utilized as a fluorescent probe of Fe3+ ion, as well as fluorescent ink and live cells imaging agent [21]. Miao et al. used the CDs derived from tomato juice to detect a tumor-associated carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), by adsorbing CEA-aptamer onto the surface of CDs [12]. Li et al. prepared CDs from Hongcaitai and used them for detecting hypochlorite and mercuric ions, as well as cell imaging [23].

Prunus cerasifera is a species of plum known by the common names cherry plum and myrobalan plum [26]. It is a popular ornamental tree for garden and landscaping use. The fruits of Prunus cerasifera are rich in color-stable anthocyanins and contain an abundance of organic acids, pectin, mineral elements, and all essential amino acids [27]. They are eaten fresh or used for soups, juices and in alcoholic beverages. However, as far as we know, the synthesis of CDs from Prunus cerasifera fruits has not been reported. Hence, we attempted to synthesize fluorescent CDs from Prunus cerasifera fruits, and explore their potential biomedical applications.

Herein, we used the Prunus cerasifera fruits as carbon source to prepare high luminescent CDs via hydrothermal method, and characterized the obtained CDs by TEM, FTIR, XPS, ultraviolet and fluorescence spectra, respectively. The CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet illumination, with excellent aqueous solubility. Their potential use as an invisible fluorescent ink was assessed, owing to their fluorescent nature. The CDs were also used to detect various metal ions, especially for selectively quenching in the present of Fe3+ ion. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity of CDs was evaluated by MTT method and the cell imaging was conducted by inverted fluorescence microscope, to evaluate their potential as cell imaging agent.

The Prunus cerasifera fruits were collected from Dalian Medical University (Dalian, China) and washed with deionized water and dried at room temperature. K2SO4, AgNO3, SnCl2·4H2O, BaCl2, Pb(NO3)2, CuSO4·5H2O, MgSO4·7H2O, MnSO4·H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, FeCl3, ZnCl2, NaCl and CaCl2 were purchased from Adamas-Beta Co., Ltd. (Adamas-Beta, Shanghai, China). Trypsin, penicillin and streptomycin were purchased from Multisciences (Lianke) Biotech Co., Ltd. 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd.
All of the chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification.

The CDs was prepared using a hydrothermal method. Briefly, fresh Prunus cerasifera fruits were washed with deionized water and dried at room temperature. The peel and kernel were removed, and the sarcocarp were cut into small pieces and juiced in the blender. After filtration, 25 mL of the obtained juice was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (Anhui Kemi Machinery Technology Co., Ltd., China) and heated at 200 °C for 20 h. After cooled down to room temperature, the obtained brown solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane. The solution was further purified by Sephadex G-25 gel filtration chromatography by using pure water as the eluent. The fractions with bright blue color fluorescence under ultraviolet light were collected, and lyophilized to obtain the desired CDs.

The fluorescence spectra of CDs were measured by a Synergy H1 Hybrid Multi-Mode Microplate Reader (BioTek, Winooski, VT, USA). The ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum of the CDs was measured by a Zolix SBP300 spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were acquired on a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) spectra were collected on an FTIR spectrophotometer (IRPrestige-21, Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurement was carried out on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo, USA).

The CDs solution was loaded into a fountain pen, and written down the letters of “DaLian DMU” on a filter paper. The letters were observed and taken photos under daylight and UV light (365 nm), respectively.

The detection of Fe3+ ion was performed on the BioTek microplate reader with a black opaque 96-well plate (Jet Bio-Filtration, Guangzhou, China). Briefly, 100 μL CDs solution with the concentration of 140 μg/mL was added into each well of the black opaque 96-well plate. Then, 100 μL metal ion solution containing 2 mM K+, Ag+, Sn2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Na+ or Ca2+ was also added into the 96-well plate. The mixture of CDs solution (final concentration of 70 μg/mL) and metal ion solution (final concentration of 1 mM) were incubated for 10 min at room temperature, and measured by BioTek microplate reader. The excitation wavelength was set at 360 nm, and the emission wavelength was set at 450 nm.

The sensitivity for Fe3+ ion detection by CDs was conducted in a similar way, where the different metal solutions were replaced with Fe3+ ion solution (0–1 mM). The fluorescence intensity of CDs at 450 nm was recorded.

The cytotoxicity of CDs was evaluated by MTT assay [28,29]. Briefly, HepG2 cells (CHI Scientific, Wuxi, China) were cultured in high glucose Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (H-DMEM, Life Technologies, Carlsbad, CA, USA) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (Sijiqing, Zhejiang Tianhang Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China) and antibiotics (penicillin, 50 U/mL; streptomycin, 50 U/mL) and incubated in 5% CO2 at 37 °C. Firstly, HepG2 cells were seeded in 96-well plates (Shengyou, Hangzhou Xinyou Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China) at density of 1 × 104 cells/well (100 μL) and incubated for 24 h. Then, CDs solution was added into each well to form the final concentration of 0, 7.8, 15.6, 31.3, 62.5, 125, 250 and 500 μg/mL. The HepG2 cells were incubated for another 24 h. After incubation, the medium was replaced with 100 μL DMEM (containing 5 mg/mL MTT), and the cells were incubated for additional 4 h. After that, the supernatant was discarded and 200 μL DMSO was added to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals. Finally, the optical density (OD) was measured at 570 nm with a microplate reader (Thermo 354, USA).

Cells without CDs were incubated as parallel negative controls, and the wells with added culture medium without cells served as blank controls for zero absorbance at the same time. The cell viability was calculated as the following equation.
(1)
Cell viability
%
=
Abs
sample
− Abs
blank
Abs
control
− Abs
blank
× 100
IC50 value, a concentration at which 50% of cells were killed, was determined on the basis of dose–response curve.

For the cell imaging test, HepG2 cells were seeded in 24-well plates at densities of 2 × 105 cell/well and incubated for 24 h. CDs solution were added into each well with the concentration of 350 μg/mL and further incubated for 12 h. Then, the culture medium was discarded and the cells were rinsed with cold PBS for at least three times. At last, the fluorescence images were obtained on a fluorescence microscope (Olympus IX81, Tokyo, Japan).

The carbon dots were prepared by hydrothermal method using Prunus cerasifera fruits as the raw material. As shown in Scheme 1 , the obtained CDs solution was dark brown and it displayed bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. The characterizations of the CDs were examined by TEM, FTIR and XPS, respectively. The TEM image in Fig. 1A showed the morphology of CDs, which were near-spherical and well separated. The diameters of CDs were ranged between 1 and 8 nm, and most of which were distributed in 3–5 nm. The FTIR spectrum in Fig. 1B showed the strong absorption peak at 3340 cm−1, which was corresponding to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds. The peak at 2930 cm−1 was due to the CH stretching of aliphatic carbon compounds. The peaks at 1672, 1597 and 1402 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO in ketone, stretching vibrations of CC and CN, respectively [16]. Moreover, the peaks at 1109 cm−1 indicated the present of ether linkage [20]. These results meant that the CDs possessed various functional groups, which was beneficial for enhancing their fluorescence property.

XPS analysis was conducted to determine the chemical structure of CDs, and the spectra were displayed in Fig. 2 . The survey spectrum (Fig. 2A) displayed three typical peaks at 284.76 eV, 399.77 eV and 531.06 eV, which was corresponding to the C 1s, N 1s and O 1s elements. The percentages of C, N and O elements in CDs were 76.25%, 4.57% and 21.18%, respectively. Furthermore, the high solution C 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three main peaks at 284.76 eV, 286.16 eV and 288.26 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CO and OCO/OCNH bonds, respectively. The high solution N 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.76 eV and 401.86 eV, which were assigned to pyridinic N (CNC) and graphitic N (C3 N) [21,30]. Similarly, the high solution C 1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks at 531.06 eV, 532.16 eV and 533.01 eV, corresponding to the bonds of CO, CO and OCO, respectively. The data demonstrated that the CDs were carbon-rich with nitrogen and oxygen. Moreover, the nitrogen atoms were successfully incorporated into the core of CDs [13].

The aqueous solution of CDs exhibited a yellowish color under daylight conditions, and displayed bright blue color under UV irradiation at 365 nm. To further understand the optical characterization of CDs, UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were measured. As seen in Fig. 3A, CDs displayed a broad UV–Vis absorption from 220 to 450 nm, with a weak peak at 285 nm, which was ascribed to the π-π⁎ transition of CC bonds. Moreover, the CDs exhibited the maximum of fluorescence emission wavelength at 450 nm, when exhibited at 360 nm, showing a general Stoke Shift of 90 nm. Fig. 3B showed the further fluorescence properties of CDs under various excited wavelengths, founding they were excitation-dependent. The emission peaks were gradually red shifted from 400 nm to 550 nm, when the excitation wavelength changed from 300 nm to 480 nm. The maximum emission wavelength was observed at 450 nm, corresponding to the maximum excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Fig. 3C and D demonstrated the pH stability and photostability of CDs, by recording the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 450 nm. The solutions with different pH values were prepared by adding hydrochloric acid and sodium hydrate solutions using a pH-Stat automatic titration unit (848 Titrino plus, Metrohm AG, Herisau, Switzerland) [31]. The results showed that CDs stable at pH 3–11, however, the intensity decreased once the pH value was below 3 or over 11. This indicated that the CDs were sensitive to strong acidic and basic conditions. The photostability results showed that the CDs were stable for over 11 days under normal storage condition.

Due to the excellent properties such as dispersibility, solubility and strong fluorescence, CDs have been applied in lots of fields. The application of fluorescent ink using CDs has been reported by several works. In this work, we tentatively studied the application of CDs as invisible fluorescent ink. The CDs aqueous solution was loaded into a pen without any modification. Then, the letters of “Dalian DMU” were handwritten on a filter paper. Fig. 4 showed the digital photographs of the letters radiated under daylight and 365 nm UV light. It was obvious that the letters were colorless under daylight, however, the bright blue fluorescence letters under UV irradiation were observed. Moreover, the resulting fluorescent ink was clear, permanent, pollution free, biocompatible and easily washable. Hence, the CDs solution might be an alternative applied in for fluorescence pens or anticounterfeit field [21,32].

The application of CDs as fluorescence sensors has been widely reported. They can be used to identify the metal ions, drugs (vitamin B2, dopamine), small molecules (H2O2, 2,4-dinitrophenol), fluoride and DNA [33–36]. In general, the detection and separation of heavy metal ions in water is necessary in business application and our daily life, and the selective fluorescence quenching effect of CDs quenched by special ion has drawn much attention in ion sensor. The application of CDs in identifying metal ions has been reported by several researches, such as Fe3+, Ag+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Ce3+, and Hg2+ [37–39]. In this work, we studied the feasibility of CDs as fluorescence sensor of metal ions. Various metal ions including K+, Ag+, Sn2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Na+ and Ca2+ with the concentrations of 1 mM were reacted with CDs at room temperature. The fluorescence intensities of CDs in the present of metal ions (F) and blank CDs (F0) at 450 nm were measured. The ratio of F/F0 could reflect the effect of fluorescence quenching by each metal ion, where the lower F/F0 value meant stronger fluorescence quenching effect. Fig. 5A displayed the F/F0 values of metal ions, and we found most ions had the F/F0 values between 0.8 and 1.2 except of Fe3+ ion. The F/F0 value of Fe3+ ion was below 0.5, meaning Fe3+ ion could selectively quench the fluorescence intensity of CDs. The high selectivity of CDs for Fe3+ ion is probably ascribed to the stronger affinity between Fe3+ ion and CDs, as well as faster chelating process of Fe3+ ion towards “N” and “O” of CDs, in comparison with other metal ions [21,32]. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presents of Fe3+ ion was reported to be caused by the electron transfer between CDs and Fe3+ ion [21]. Furthermore, the relationship of between the fluorescence quenching of CDs and the concentrations of Fe3+ ion was conducted. As seen in Fig. 5B, the function of (F0 − F)/F0 presented the degree of quenched CDs, which was increased with the increase of Fe3+ ion concentration (0–1 mM). The insert Fig. 5B showed a good linear relationship between the (F0 − F)/F0 value and Fe3+ ion concentration (R2 = 0.9973), when the concentration of Fe3+ ion was below 0.5 mM. This indicated that CDs could quantitatively reflect the concentration of Fe3+ ion within the wide range of 0–0.5 mM. In addition, the digital photographs of CDs with or without Fe3+ ion radiated under daylight and UV light were listed in Fig. 5C. It was obvious that the CDs aqueous solution was bright blue color under UV irradiation. However, the blue color was disappeared in the present of Fe3+ ion, which also proved that the CDs were quenched by Fe3+ ion. Hence, the results strongly suggested that CDs were selectively quenched by Fe3+ ion, and they could be developed as the Fe3+ ion fluorescence sensor. The sensor might be useful for detection of Fe3+ ion from industrial waste.

The biological applications of CDs have been widely reported, such as drug delivery, biosensors and bioimaging. This requires the CDs should meet the requirement of low cytotoxicity and bright intracellular signal. To evaluate the potential of CDs on cell imaging, we firstly studied the cellular toxicity of CDs by MTT method. CDs with different concentrations were incubated with HepG2 cells for 24 h, and the cellular viabilities were shown in Fig. 6 . We found the cellular viability was higher than 90% when the concentration of CDs was below 500 μg/mL. It demonstrated that CDs exerted low toxicity to HepG2 cells, and could be potentially used for cell imaging and other biomedical application. Furthermore, the cell imaging study was performed by a fluorescence microscope. HepG2 cell were incubated with 350 μg/mL CDs and the images under bright field and 405/488 nm excitation were captured. As shown in Fig. 7 , the CDs treated cells could emit blue and green fluorescence under irradiation, revealing the CDs could penetrate the cell membrane and spread into the cytoplasm and the nuclei. Similar to some other reported CDs, the CDs prepared from Prunus cerasifera fruits could also exhibit multicolor fluorescence in cellular imaging. Hence, as derived from natural products and synthesized by green approach, the CDs exhibited low-toxicity and good biocompatibility to cells, as well as the capability of cell imaging.

In this work, Prunus cerasifera fruits were first used as carbon sources to fabricate the CDs via hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs could emit bright, stable and excitation-dependent blue fluorescence in aqueous solution under irradiation. The aqueous solutions of CDs could be applied as fluorescent ink, as the letters written on the paper by CDs solution showed bright fluorescence on UV irradiation. Moreover, the CDs could be used as the fluorescent probe of Fe3+ ion, as they could be selectively quenched by Fe3+ ion and the degree of quenched CDs could reflect the concentration of Fe3+ ion. At last, the CDs were found low-toxicity to HepG2 cells and they could be applied as the bioimaging agent to label the cells. In summary, we the CDs prepared from Prunus cerasifera fruits exhibited excellent fluorescence properties, and could be potentially applied in the field of fluorescent ink, Fe3+ ion detection and cell imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 81703606, 81601825), the Educational Committee Foundation of Liaoning Province (grant no. L2016026), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (grant no. wd01185).
